desc: Hangzhou is one of China's most polished big-city breaks for travelers who want classical scenery without giving up urban comfort. West Lake, temple hills, tea villages, and a long cultural afterlife make it feel more poetic and landscaped than a business-first metropolis, though the city is still large enough that humidity, holiday surges, and transport scale shape the trip.
hls: West Lake is the emotional center of the city and gives Hangzhou a softer, more cultural identity than many Chinese megacities.
The classic Hangzhou mix is water, hills, pagodas, tea culture, and Buddhism rather than skyscraper sightseeing alone.
Lingyin Temple adds a major spiritual and historical stop that feels integrated with the wooded landscape, not detached from it.
Longjing tea villages and surrounding slopes make the city feel grounded in a famous local product rather than generic urban tourism.
Spring and autumn are the clearest prime windows; summer is lush but hot, humid, and heavier with domestic demand.
Hangzhou works best when you give it enough time to slow down around the lake instead of treating it as a rushed Shanghai side trip.

todo.name: West Lake circuit by foot and boat
todo.desc: Use the lake properly rather than as a quick viewpoint, because it is the defining landscape that gives Hangzhou its identity.

todo.name: Lingyin Temple and Feilai Peak area
todo.desc: Set aside a substantial block for Lingyin, where temple history, wooded hills, and rock carvings all matter to the experience.

todo.name: Leifeng Pagoda at dusk
todo.desc: Time this for later in the day if possible, since the pagoda's setting over West Lake is part of the classic Hangzhou image.

todo.name: Longjing or Meijiawu tea village visit
todo.desc: Use one half-day for tea-country atmosphere so Hangzhou feels tied to its most famous cultural product.

todo.name: Lakeside evening performance or stroll
todo.desc: Keep one evening for the lake after dark, when the city leans hardest into its romantic and theatrical side.

todo.name: Wetland or museum extension
todo.desc: Add Xixi or a museum if you want Hangzhou to read as a fuller city break instead of only a West Lake postcard.

wifi.note: Usually strong in major hotels and apartments, but international app access can still require planning around local internet restrictions.

prac.fltNote: Hangzhou has a major airport, but not a practical nonstop from the repo's London origins. Current long-haul service from Hangzhou includes routes such as Lisbon and Madrid rather than London, so UK trips are connection-led via other European, Gulf, or Chinese hubs by default.
prac.visa: China entry rules vary by passport and current policy, so verify visa or transit eligibility before travel.
alerts: Chinese national-holiday periods, especially early October, can transform key sights and transport lines.
Late spring and summer bring real humidity and heavier rain, which changes how long outdoor lake loops feel.
Some international services and payment flows can be less frictionless for foreign visitors than hotel marketing suggests.

sweet: March to May and late September to November are usually Hangzhou's strongest windows for lake scenery, temple visits, and tea-country day trips without the worst summer humidity.
